name: Donald Trump
summary: Donald Trump's character and pattern of behavior are marked by a history of discriminatory practices, financial mismanagement, and disregard for the law. He has been involved in over 4,000 lawsuits and has filed for bankruptcy multiple times, often leaving creditors and contractors unpaid. Trump has also been found liable for sexual abuse and defamation, and has been convicted of falsifying business records. His presidency was marked by numerous scandals, including the impeachment for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress, and his administration's policies and actions have been characterized as racist, sexist, and authoritarian. Trump's behavior has consistently demonstrated a lack of respect for the rule of law, the judiciary, and the principles of democracy.

entries.fact: Donald Trump countersued the U.S. government for $100 million over charges that his properties had discriminated against Black applicants and tenants, with the help of Roy Cohn.
Donald Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for six of his businesses, including the Plaza Hotel in Manhattan and the casinos in Atlantic City, New Jersey.
Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for the Trump Taj Mahal casino, personally guaranteeing future performance under the restructuring agreement.
The Plaza Hotel, owned by Donald Trump, filed for bankruptcy protection, and a reorganization plan was approved a month later, with the banks taking control of the property.
Trump's Castle and Plaza casinos filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection.

Donald Trump, working with several relatives, formed a shell company to pay vendors providing services and supplies for Trump's rental units, then billed those services and supplies to Trump Management with significant markups, inflating rents and transferring assets from Fred Trump to his children and nephew.
Trump founded Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR), which assumed ownership of the Trump Plaza.
Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR) went bankrupt, leaving Trump with 10% ownership.
Trump declared the Trump International Hotel and Tower in Chicago to be worthless on his tax return, despite later reporting income from the property.
Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR) went bankrupt again.
The State of New York filed a $40 million civil suit against Trump University, alleging that the company made false statements and defrauded consumers.
NBC and Univision dropped the Miss Universe pageants in reaction to Trump's comments about Mexican immigrants.

Donald Trump announced his candidacy for the 2016 presidential election, claiming media bias against him and campaigning as a rich, successful businessman and an outsider without political experience.
Donald Trump described NATO as 'obsolete' and espoused noninterventionist and protectionist views.
The New York attorney general's office ordered the Trump Foundation to cease its fundraising activities in New York immediately, citing violations of state law.
Donald Trump had been involved in more than 4,000 lawsuits, liens, and other filings, often filed for nonpayment against him by employees, contractors, real estate brokers, and his own attorneys.
The New York attorney general's office filed a civil suit against the Trump Foundation, Trump, and his adult children, seeking $2.8 million in restitution and additional penalties.
Trump was impeached for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress.
Trump declared Mar-a-Lago as his primary residence.
A New York state judge ordered Trump to pay $2 million to a group of charities for misusing the Trump Foundation's funds, in part to finance his presidential campaign.
Trump downplayed the severity of the COVID-19 pandemic, contradicted health officials, and signed the CARES Act in response to the pandemic.
Trump was impeached for incitement of insurrection.
Trump's attempt to overturn the 2020 presidential election result culminated in the January 6 Capitol attack.
Trump was found liable in civil cases for sexual abuse and defamation and for business fraud.
Trump was found guilty in 34 counts of falsifying business records, making him the first U.S. president convicted of a felony.
Trump initiated mass layoffs of federal workers at the start of his second presidency.
Trump imposed tariffs on nearly all countries at the highest level since the Great Depression and signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

Trump's administration targeted political opponents and civil society, persecuted transgender people, and conducted mass deportation of immigrants, resulting in over 550 lawsuits challenging their legality.

The Internal Revenue Service was investigating whether Trump had twice written off losses incurred through construction cost overruns and lagging sales of residential units in the Trump International Hotel and Tower in Chicago.
Trump authorized a joint U.S./Israeli war with Iran that resulted in the killing of Iran's leader.
Donald Trump claimed Mexican immigrants were 'bringing drugs, they're bringing crime, they're rapists' in his campaign launch speech.
Donald Trump made 'explicitly racist and sexist appeals to win over white voters' during his 2016 presidential campaign.
The FBI launched Crossfire Hurricane, an investigation into possible links between Russia and Trump's 2016 campaign.
Trump rejects the scientific consensus on climate change.
He reduced the budget for renewable energy research by 40 percent and reversed Obama-era policies directed at curbing climate change.
He withdrew from the Paris Agreement, making the U.S. the only nation to not ratify it.
Trump rolled back more than 100 federal environmental regulations, including those that curbed greenhouse gas emissions, air and water pollution, and the use of toxic substances.

Trump dismantled federal regulations on health, labor, the environment, and other areas, including a bill that revoked the Obama-era regulation restricting the sale of firearms to severely mentally ill people.
Trump delayed, suspended, or reversed ninety federal regulations, often after requests by the regulated industries.
Trump described illegal immigration as an 'invasion' of the United States and drastically escalated immigration enforcement.
Trump reduced the number of refugees admitted to record lows, from an annual limit of 110,000 before he took office to 15,000 in 2021.

Trump signed six executive orders, including authorizing procedures for repealing the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act ("Obamacare"), withdrawal from the Trans-Pacific Partnership negotiations, advancement of the Keystone XL and Dakota Access Pipeline projects, and planning for a wall along the U.S. border with Mexico.
Trump signed an executive order to scale back the implementation of the Affordable Care Act.
Trump signed an executive order that denied entry to citizens from six Muslim-majority countries for four months and from Syria indefinitely.
Trump replaced the initial travel ban with a presidential proclamation extending travel bans to North Koreans, Chadians, and some Venezuelan officials.
The Supreme Court allowed the revised travel ban to go into effect.
Trump signed the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, which reduced tax rates for businesses and individuals and eliminated the penalty associated with the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate.
Trump signed legislation to increase funding for drug treatments in response to the opioid epidemic, but was widely criticized for failing to make a concrete strategy.
Trump barred organizations that provide abortions or abortion referrals from receiving federal funds.
Trump's administration had a policy of family separation that separated over 4,400 children from migrant parents at the U.S.-Mexico border.
Trump mandated that illegal immigrant families be detained together unless there was a concern of risk for the child, after initially blaming Democrats for the family separation policy.
Trump's refusal to sign any spending bill unless it allocated funding for the border wall resulted in the longest-ever federal government shutdown, for 35 days from December 2018 to January 2019.
Under Trump, the federal budget deficit increased by almost 50 percent, to nearly $1 trillion in 2019.
Trump's administration took an anti-marijuana position, revoking Obama-era policies that provided protections for states that legalized marijuana.
Trump's administration oversaw the federal government execute 13 prisoners, more than in the previous 56 years combined, ending a 17-year moratorium.
Trump's administration rolled back key components of the Obama administration's workplace protections against discrimination of LGBTQ people.
A federal judge halted Trump's attempted rollback of anti-discrimination protections for transgender patients.
By the end of his term, the U.S. national debt increased by 39 percent, reaching $27.75 trillion, and the U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio hit a post-World War II high.
Trump resigned from SAG-AFTRA to avoid a disciplinary hearing regarding the January 6 attack, and two days later, the union permanently barred him.
Trump granted 73 pardons and commuted 70 sentences in his last full day in office.
Three U.S. intelligence agencies jointly stated with 'high confidence' that Russia interfered in the 2016 presidential election to favor Trump.
Trump disparaged courts and judges he disagreed with, often in personal terms, and questioned the judiciary's constitutional authority.
Trump dismissed Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) director James Comey, saying a few days later that he was concerned about Comey's role in the Trump-Russia investigations.
Trump contradicted the official White House account of the Comey firing.
A judge ordered that the families be reunited and further separations stopped except in limited circumstances.
The Supreme Court upheld the travel ban.
Mueller's final report found that Russia interfered in 2016 to favor Trump and that Trump and his campaign welcomed and encouraged the effort.
Trump pressured Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy to investigate Joe and Hunter Biden, in an attempt to gain an advantage in the 2020 presidential election.
The House voted to impeach Trump for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress.
Trump initially ignored public health warnings and calls for action from health officials within his administration regarding the COVID-19 pandemic.
Trump described himself as a 'nationalist' and his foreign policy as 'America First'.
The Senate acquitted Trump in his first impeachment trial.
Trump halted funding of the World Health Organization in response to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Trump's campaign focused on crime, claiming that cities would descend into lawlessness if Democratic nominee Joe Biden won.
Trump hosted the signing of the Abraham Accords between Israel and the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain to normalize their foreign relations.
Trump declared victory before the results were known on the morning after the election.
Trump baselessly alleged election fraud after Joe Biden was projected the winner.
Trump held a rally at which he called for the election to be overturned and urged his supporters to 'fight like hell' and 'take back our country' by marching to the Capitol.
The House charged Trump with incitement of insurrection for his role in the January 6 Capitol attack.
The Senate acquitted Trump in his second impeachment trial.
Trump began using the term 'big lie' to refer to the 2020 election itself, after initially having his false claims about the election referred to as the 'big lie' by his critics.
The National Archives and Records Administration retrieved 15 boxes of documents Trump had taken to Mar-a-Lago after leaving the White House, some of which were classified.
Trump took credit for the overturning of Roe v. Wade by the Supreme Court, after appointing three conservative justices to the court.
FBI agents searched Mar-a-Lago for illegally held documents, including those in breach of the Espionage Act, collecting 11 sets of classified documents, some marked top secret.

The U.S. House committee on the January 6 attack recommended criminal charges against Trump for obstructing an official proceeding, inciting or assisting an insurrection, and conspiracy to defraud the United States.
Trump's campaign began diverting 10% of donations to his leadership PAC.
A federal grand jury constituted by Special Counsel Jack Smith indicted Trump on 31 counts of 'willfully retaining national defense information' under the Espionage Act, among other charges.
A grand jury in Fulton County, Georgia, indicted Trump on 13 charges, including racketeering, for his efforts to subvert the 2020 election in the state.

Trump was convicted on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records, stemming from evidence that he booked Michael Cohen's hush-money payments to adult film actress Stormy Daniels as business expenses to cover up his alleged 2006-2007 affair with Daniels during the 2016 election.
Trump was found liable for sexual abuse and defamation and ordered to pay $5 million in one case and $83.3 million in the other, in lawsuits filed by journalist E. Jean Carroll.
Trump's politics and rhetoric led to the formation of a political movement known as Trumpism, which has been compared to a cult of personality.
Trump's comments and actions have been characterized as racist, including his explicit and routine disparagement of racial, religious, and ethnic minorities.
Trump made increasingly violent and authoritarian statements during his campaign.
Trump's campaign had paid $100 million towards his legal bills.
Trump was shot in the ear in an assassination attempt at a campaign rally in Butler Township, Pennsylvania.
Federal appeals courts upheld the findings and awards in the E. Jean Carroll lawsuits, ordering Trump to pay $5 million and $83.3 million.
Trump announced his candidacy for the 2024 presidential election and created a fundraising account.

Trump maintained a publicly traded company in Trump Media & Technology Group, and diversified it into financial services, while also pursuing new overseas real estate deals involving state-affiliated entities and having several branding and licensing deals selling Trump-branded merchandise

Trump launched, promoted, and personally benefited from two cryptocurrency tokens ('meme coins'), $Trump and $Melania, and directly benefited from his family's cryptocurrency company World Liberty Financial, which engaged in an unprecedented mixing of private enterprise and government policy
Trump ordered agencies to stop enforcement of disfavored rules in an attempt at large-scale deregulation that legal experts described as illegal and contrary to decades of federal law.

Trump sought greater government control over private businesses and shifted away from traditional conservative free market orthodoxy, engaging in state capitalism by taking direct government equity stakes in multiple U.S. corporations.

Trump canceled and paused federal grants and made large cuts to scientific research, several of which were found by judges and the Government Accountability Office as being illegal and unconstitutional.
Trump and his administration's Make America Healthy Again agenda promoted anti-science and anti-vaccine activism, resulting in a resurgence of whooping cough and measles.
Trump relied on declaring national emergencies to justify hundreds of actions and bypass congressional approval or regulatory review.
Trump declared a national energy emergency, allowing the suspension of environmental regulations, loosening the rules for fossil fuel extraction and limiting renewable energy projects.
Trump began his second term upon his inauguration, becoming the oldest to assume the presidency and the first president with a felony conviction.
Trump issued more executive orders than any other U.S. president's first 100 days.
Trump implemented a hiring freeze across the federal government and ordered telework of federal employees to be discontinued within 30 days
Trump ordered a review of many career civil service positions with the intention of reclassifying them into at-will positions without job protections

Trump initiated mass job terminations of federal employees, which were described by legal experts as unprecedented or in violation of federal law, with the intent of replacing them with workers more aligned with his agenda

Trump ordered an end to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) projects in the federal government and rescinded Lyndon B. Johnson's 1965 Executive Order 11246, which had mandated that federal contractors take affirmative action to end racial discrimination

Trump and Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency largely dismantled several federal agencies, including USAID and the Department of Education, unilaterally fired several thousand staff, and reduced administrative functions to statutory minimums
Trump threatened, signed executive actions, and ordered investigations into his political opponents, critics, and organizations aligned with the Democratic Party

Trump's actions against civil society were described by hundreds of legal experts and political scientists as authoritarian, contributing to democratic backsliding, and negatively impacting the rule of law

Trump granted clemency to all January 6 rioters convicted or charged on his first day in office, including those who violently attacked police, by pardoning more than 1,500 and commuting the sentences of 14
Trump defied judges and the courts in roughly one third of all cases against him, actions which were described by legal experts as unprecedented for any presidential administration
Trump's orders and actions had been challenged in over 550 lawsuits nationwide, with plaintiffs prevailing in 195 cases and the government prevailing in 109 cases
Donald Trump divorced Ivana Zelníčková following his affair with model and actress Marla Maples.
Donald Trump bragged about kissing and groping women without their consent in a 2005 recording, saying 'when you're a star, they let you do it. You can do anything. ... Grab 'em by the pussy'

Trump condemned the white supremacist demonstrators and the counter-protesters at the Unite the Right rally, stating that there were 'very fine people on both sides', implying a moral equivalence between the two groups.
Trump reportedly referred to El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, and African nations as 'shithole countries', remarks condemned as racist.
The House of Representatives condemned Trump for racist remarks about four minority Democratic congresswomen.
Trump attributed societal problems to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives and wokeness, equating diversity with incompetence.
Trump reversed pro-diversity policies in the federal government.
Trump threatened cultural institutions and sixty universities on DEI and antisemitism grounds, and forced law firms to capitulate to his political agenda.
Trump expanded the domestic use of the military and ordered military deployments to several Democratic Party-led cities, and threatened to expand his deployments further.

Trump banned transgender people from the military, restricted or defunded gender-affirming healthcare, opposed inclusive language, censored research and education materials, targeted schools, universities, and cultural institutions accused of promoting what his government calls 'gender ideology', barred transgender athletes from sports, and required U.S. passports to state transgender people's sex assigned at birth.

Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Paris Agreement on climate change during his first presidency, Biden reentered the agreement in 2021, and Trump again withdrew from the agreement during his second presidency.
Trump resumed the Remain in Mexico policy, designated drug cartels as terrorist groups, and ordered construction to be resumed on a border wall.
Trump revoked the parole status of immigrants who entered the U.S. under CBP One and ended humanitarian parole for Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans.
Trump targeted activists, legal immigrants, tourists, and students with visas who expressed criticism of his policies or pro-Palestinian advocacy.
Several American citizens were detained and deported.
Trump used the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 to imprison illegal immigrants without trial at the Terrorism Confinement Center in El Salvador.

Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act into law, making the temporary tax cuts of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act permanent and adding additional tax deductions for a total of around $4.5 trillion, mostly benefiting the highest income brackets and costing people in the lowest income bracket $1,600 per year.
Trump imposed tariffs on most countries, including large tariffs on major trading partners China, Canada, and Mexico.
Trump threatened and enacted tariffs against treaty allies for opposing his political objectives.
Trump pursued legally controversial military strikes against alleged drug boats in international waters.
Trump and Vice President Vance berated Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy in a highly contentious televised meeting.
Trump ordered the bombing of Iranian nuclear sites.
Trump's plan for a Gaza ceasefire deal between Israel and Hamas was signed, leading to the creation of the Board of Peace.
Trump pardoned former Honduran president Juan Orlando Hernández, who had been extradited to the U.S. in 2022 and sentenced to 45 years in prison for drug trafficking.
The U.S. captured Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro in a military raid in Venezuela and took him to New York, where he was charged with drug trafficking.
Trump's aggressive and hardline deportation campaign led to large nationwide protests and violent confrontations with migrants and protesters.
Trump authorized joint U.S./Israeli air strikes on Iran.
The Supreme Court ruled the tariffs Trump imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to be illegal.
Trump selected cabinet members with personal loyalty to him, with the 'focus on loyalty over subject-matter expertise'.
Trump threatened the annexation of Greenland, including by military force or the imposition of tariffs on European allies.
Donald Trump promoted the racist 'birther' conspiracy theory that Barack Obama, the first black U.S. president, was not born in the United States
Forbes estimated Donald Trump's net worth at $4.5 billion, based on interviews with more than 80 sources
Donald Trump made over 30,573 false or misleading statements during his first presidency, according to The Washington Post's fact-checkers
Trump promoted conspiracy theories such as climate change denial, alleged Ukrainian interference in U.S. elections, and vaccines and autism
Donald Trump characterized his 2005 comments about kissing and groping women as 'locker-room talk'
Donald Trump's longtime personal physician, Harold Bornstein, said Trump had dictated the contents of a letter stating that he would 'be the healthiest individual ever elected to the presidency'
Three of Trump's agents seized Harold Bornstein's medical records in a raid on Bornstein's office
Trump issued an executive order scaling back parts of Obamacare
Donald Trump issued an executive order imposing a travel ban on citizens of several countries, causing chaos and protests at US airports
Trump made it easier for the mentally ill to get guns when he rolled back an Obama regulation
Donald Trump signed a new travel ban directive, which was later halted by a federal judge in Hawaii
Donald Trump had potential conflicts of interest due to his business dealings

Trump signed a health care executive order that allowed small businesses and associations to band together to buy health insurance plans that might not meet all the requirements of the Affordable Care Act
The American Political Science Association ranked Donald Trump the worst president

At least 26 women have publicly accused Donald Trump of sexual misconduct, including rape, kissing without consent, groping, looking under women's skirts, and walking in on naked teenage pageant contestants

Donald Trump's rhetoric is associated with an increased incidence of hate crimes, and he has an emboldening effect on expressing prejudicial attitudes due to his normalization of explicit racial rhetoric
Trump frequently accused the press of bias, calling it the 'fake news media' and 'the enemy of the people'

Federal law-enforcement officials used tear gas and other crowd control tactics to remove a largely peaceful crowd of lawful protesters from Lafayette Square, outside the White House, before Trump posed with a Bible for a photo op at the nearby St. John's Episcopal Church

Donald Trump refused to condemn the white supremacist Proud Boys during a presidential debate, and his comment 'Proud Boys, stand back and stand by' was said to have led to increased recruitment for the pro-Trump group
Trump tried and failed to overturn the election with voter fraud claims
Trump was banned from Twitter after the January 6 attack
Trump was banned from Facebook and Instagram after the January 6 attack
Trump launched social media platform Truth Social
Twitter reinstated Trump's account
The American Political Science Association again ranked Donald Trump the worst president
Trump repeatedly voiced support for outlawing political dissent and criticism
Trump's 2024 campaign made extensive use of dehumanizing language and racial stereotypes
Forbes estimated Donald Trump's net worth at $5.1 billion and ranked him the 700th wealthiest person in the world
Donald Trump's administration did not release files relating to Jeffrey Epstein, despite Trump's promise to do so during the 2024 campaign
Roughly three million additional pages of files related to Jeffrey Epstein were released, including approximately 38,000 references to Trump, his wife, Mar-a-Lago, and other Trump-related terms
Donald Trump raised birther questions about President Obama
Donald Trump entertained conspiracy theories
The Supreme Court upheld Donald Trump's travel ban, allowing it to go into full effect
Trump said he plans to 'let Obamacare fail'
Trump cut funding for Affordable Care Act enrollment programs by 40 percent
The United States became the only country to reject the Paris climate agreement under Trump's leadership
Donald Trump signed a bill to temporarily reopen the government after a record-long shutdown, which was caused in part by his demands for border wall funding
Donald Trump declared a national emergency to build a border wall, setting up a massive legal fight
Trump proposed cuts to climate and clean-energy programs
The Trump administration cut refugee admissions to a record low, effectively ending the US role as a worldwide leader on refugees
Trump approves sanctions on Russia gas pipeline
The Trump administration sidelined researchers and their work on climate change and other scientific issues
Trump spurned science on climate change, saying 'don't think science knows'
Trump refused to commit to a peaceful transition of power, prompting the Pentagon to stress it would play no role in the election.
The Trump administration virtually cut off refugees, with Donald Trump unleashing a tirade on immigrants
The Trump administration sabotaged the Affordable Care Act in six major ways
Trump declared victory in the presidential election despite results from key states being unclear.
Trump revived baseless claims of election fraud after Biden won the presidential race.
The Trump administration rolled out 'midnight regulations' that undermined various policies, including environmental and health care regulations
The Supreme Court rejected a Texas lawsuit seeking to subvert the election.
Trump pressured the Georgia secretary of state to recalculate the vote in his favor during an hour-long call.
Trump told supporters to 'fight' before a mob stormed the Capitol, resulting in several deaths.
Trump's words at a rally were seen as incitement to riot before the mob stormed the Capitol.
The Trump administration's national debt increased significantly, which will weigh down the economy for years
The Trump administration carried out its 13th and final execution, as part of a rush of federal executions in Donald Trump's final days in office
The Trump administration rolled back more than 100 environmental rules
Trump's administration failed to deliver on several promises, including bringing back coal jobs
Trump urged a Wisconsin legislator to overturn his 2020 defeat in the state.
Trump proposed a plan to 'clean out' Gaza and suggested that Jordan and Egypt should accept more Palestinian refugees.
Trump's administration continued to hollow out the federal workforce, firing more than 30,000 employees
Donald Trump made false allegations against Obama, including 'treason'
Donald Trump has a history of promoting conspiracy theories
Donald Trump tried to overturn the election with voter fraud claims
Donald Trump escalated rhetoric on outlawing political dissent and criticism, calling it 'totally illegal'
Trump rebuffed NATO and aligned himself with Russia, the principal threat to the alliance.
Trump's tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China took effect.
President Trump said he 'brought back free speech', but his first 100 days told a different story
Trump widened the trade war with new tariffs, which had no precedent in the modern era.
Trump's moves against the media mirror approaches by authoritarian leaders to silence dissent
Trump announced a 10% tariff on 8 European countries for opposing US control of Greenland.
A court ruling was made in the case of Brutus v. Trump
A court ruling was made in the case of Schiff v. Trump
A court ruling was made in the case of State of Oregon v. Trump
Trump said Ukraine must reach a deal with Russia fast before trilateral talks
A court ruled against Richmond Capital Group LLC, a company associated with Donald Trump, in the case People v. Richmond Capital Group LLC.
A court ruled against Donald Trump in the case Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump.
The Supreme Court ruled that Trump's sweeping emergency tariffs were illegal.
A court case, Pacito v. Trump, was filed against Donald Trump on this date.
A court case, Fritz Emmanuel Lesly Miot v. Donald Trump, was filed

President Trump launched strikes on Iran, with his administration later clarifying that the goal is Iran's 'unconditional surrender', defined as when Iran no longer poses a threat to the US and the mission's goals have been fully realized.
President Trump's unfounded claim that Tylenol could be linked to autism in children resulted in a notable drop in orders for the medication among pregnancy-related emergency department visits

The Department of Justice released documents from the Epstein files containing sexual assault allegations against Trump, including notes from FBI interviews with a woman who alleges she was assaulted by Trump as a minor.
Trump announced a new coalition to counter drug trafficking and cartels during the inaugural 'Shield of the Americas' summit in Miami, which may lead to increased militarization of the region.
Trump falsely stated that the 2015 nuclear deal gave Iran the right to secure nuclear weapons, a claim rated False by PolitiFact.
Trump is reportedly considering an order requiring financial institutions to check customers' citizenship, which could erode trust in financial services and raise constitutional concerns.
Trump vowed to press on with U.S. military operations against Iran, declaring the country would be 'hit very hard'
Trump warned that Iran would soon 'be hit very hard' and that the U.S. may expand its target list to 'new areas and groups of people'
Trump's decision to launch a war against Iran threw the region into chaos, stranding tens of thousands of Americans
Trump falsely accused Iran of bombing a girls' school, contradicting reports that the explosions were likely caused by US airstrikes.
President Trump responded to rising fears of retaliation from Iran on the U.S. military by saying 'I guess' when asked if Americans should be concerned about a potential strike on the homeland.
Trump vowed 'no deal' with Iran except 'unconditional surrender', calling for the selection of a new leader and the end of the war launched by the US and Israel.
A court ruled against Donald Trump in the case American Federation of Government Employees, Afl-Cio v. Trump.
Multiple states sued over Trump's new global tariffs imposed after his Supreme Court loss.
Trump pressured Colorado Gov. Polis to release Tina Peters from prison amid her conviction in a scheme to find proof of fraud in the 2020 presidential election.
Trump chided the Supreme Court, saying it hasn't had the 'guts to do what's right' in response to recent decisions rejecting his policies.
Trump dismissed Iran's security chief's retaliation threats, saying 'I couldn't care less'
Trump vowed to 'take care of Cuba' in front of an assembled meeting of Latin American leaders.
A court ruled against Trump in the case Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump
A court ruled against Donald Trump in the case Lesly Miot v. Trump
President Trump claimed that former President Biden and his aides illegally used an autopen to sign official documents, prompting a DOJ probe that was later dropped due to lack of evidence

Trump threatened to not sign any bills into law until the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) America Act is approved by the Senate, which aims to change voting requirements ahead of the midterm elections
